{"step_by_step_thinking": "Tumor-associated macrophages (TAMs) are a key component of the tumor microenvironment and are known to promote tumor growth by suppressing antitumor immune responses. Document [7] explicitly states that in lung cancer, alveolar macrophages can adopt a tumor-promoting phenotype and become TAMs, which facilitate tumor growth by suppressing antitumor immune responses. It further notes that modulating the activity of these macrophages to restore their antitumor function is a promising area in cancer immunotherapy. Document [13] also lists tumor-associated macrophages (TAMs) as a potential biomarker and therapeutic target in ovarian cancer. These findings indicate that TAMs are not only present in the tumor microenvironment but are actively involved in promoting cancer progression. Therefore, targeting TAMs to reverse their immunosuppressive function is considered a promising therapeutic strategy. This supports the conclusion that TAMs are good anticancer therapeutic targets.", "answer_choice": "A"}
